rationale: The cited paper is used for more than a passing mention: the 2018 article directly invokes Ghosh and John (2005) to support its argument that specific investments can create value-claiming difficulties and ongoing haggling, which feeds into its hypothesis logic on ex post transaction costs. The citing paper also references it when positioning its contribution as adding a dynamic, longitudinal view of investment accumulation relative to prior work. This gives the citation a clear conceptual framing role, even though the 2018 study does not reuse the 2005 model or estimation approach.
relation_description: The citing paper draws on the cited paper as prior conceptual and empirical support that relationship-specific investments can generate ex post value-claiming/transaction-cost problems. It then builds its own governance-dynamics framing around when such investment effects are motivated and safeguarded over time.
